summary: Charity Majors advises an engineer who became the most senior person on their team by default (at their first job) to leave and start over elsewhere. She argues that true seniority comes not from deep familiarity with one system, but from having learned and leveled up across multiple teams, stacks, and organizations over 5-8 years.
key_points: Being the most experienced person on a single team doesn't make you a senior engineer — it just means you're familiar with that system
True seniority requires 2-3 different teams, stacks, and roles over 5-8 years
Starting over as a beginner is humbling and clarifying, and builds the skill of learning itself
Experiencing how different organizations solve problems teaches you to reason about tradeoffs rather than clinging to one blessed path
Building teams where beginners aren't miserable requires leaders who remember what it's like to start fresh
money_quotes: There is a world of distance between being expert in *this system* and being an actual expert in your chosen craft. The second is seniority; the first is merely .. familiarity
Think of every job like an escalator — a 50-foot high escalator that takes about two years to ride to the top. But once you've summited, you stall out.
Nobody wants to work with seniors who clutch their authority with a white knuckled grip. We want to work with those who wear it lightly, who remember what it was like in our shoes.
It is *good* for us to start over with beginner eyes. It's humbling, it's clarifying, it's a cleanse for the soul.
You will have proven to yourself that your success wasn't just a one-off, that you can be dropped into any situation, learn the local ropes and succeed. You will be a senior engineer.
key_insight: True engineering seniority isn't earned by becoming the expert on one system — it's forged by starting over multiple times across different teams and stacks until you can succeed anywhere.
